title: Investors warn on ‘anti-aspiration’ tax changes
full_text:
Leading investors have hit out at looming capital gains tax changes and warned of the risks posed to Australia’s start-up sector, as they called on the federal government to provide support for start-ups and venture capital in the budget.
The Albanese government is expected to announce sweeping tax reforms on investments when it hands down the federal budget on Tuesday, including abolishing the 50 per cent discount on capital gains in favour of one indexed to inflation.
The government’s reasoning for the looming changes is expected to centre around growing intergenerational inequity, but investors say the reforms pose a risk to the nation’s start-up community.
“Any changes to capital gains tax settings should be carefully assessed for their broader impact on investment behaviour, particularly in sectors like venture capital and start-ups where Australia is competing globally for capital, talent and innovation,” Hostplus chief executive David Elia said. “There is a legitimate policy question around ensuring tax settings do not unintentionally discourage investment into emerging Australian companies or contribute to capital flowing offshore.” Hostplus, the $140bn super fund for the hospitality industry, is one of Australia’s largest investors in venture capital.
Fund manager Geoff Wilson railed at the CGT changes, warning they would become “one of the biggest anti-aspiration economic reforms Australia had seen in decades”.
“Start-ups and venture capital run on the willingness of people to take enormous long-term risks in the hope of building something valuable,” he said. “If you dramatically reduce the reward for that risk, capital, founders and talent will simply move offshore.” The changes would only push more capital into passive assets and offshore markets “at precisely the time Australia desperately needs more investment in productive businesses, technology and growth”, Mr Wilson said. “You cannot tax your way to a more innovative economy. You have to incentivise people to take risks, build companies and create jobs here in Australia.” Treasurer Jim Chalmers and Prime Minister Anthony Albanese have refused to confirm the details of the changes. “I think it’s really clear to a lot of Australians that the housing market and the tax system are making it harder for people, particularly for younger people,” he said. “I think we’ve got an obligation and a responsibility to respond to that in one way or another.”
summary: Investors are concerned about proposed capital gains tax reforms that could negatively impact Australia's start-up sector and venture capital investments.

heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: The Albanese government plans to abolish the 50% discount on capital gains tax in favour of an inflation-indexed model.
Investors warn that these changes could deter investment in start-ups and lead to capital flight.
Treasurer Jim Chalmers acknowledges the need for tax reforms to address intergenerational inequity.
relevance: These proposed tax changes could significantly affect investment strategies and economic growth, which are critical considerations for actuaries and financial services professionals.
